primary_diagnosis.name: Acute severe asthma / reactive-airways exacerbation (environmental irritant-triggered), reliever-refractory, with concurrent acute irritant contact dermatitis, on a background of chronic environmental exposure and associated health anxiety (psychological factors affecting a medical condition)

comorbidities.name: Psychological factors affecting a medical condition / health anxiety about environmental exposure

pathophysiology_summary: Inhaled industrial irritants (and a background of chronic exposure) trigger bronchial smooth-muscle constriction, airway inflammation and mucus production, producing reversible airflow obstruction — an acute asthma / reactive-airways exacerbation. Tonight it is SEVERE and reliever-refractory: repeated short-acting beta-agonist (salbutamol ~6 doses in 2 hours) has given minimal relief, with tachypnoea (RR 24), hypoxaemia (SpO2 94%), tachycardia (HR 110) and accessory-muscle use (intercostal retractions) — features of a severe exacerbation at risk of progressing to respiratory fatigue and hypoxic respiratory failure. The same irritant contact drives an acute inflammatory dermatitis. Crucially, the patient's genuine, well-founded distress about environmental exposure is a red herring that must NOT anchor the assessment to anxiety/panic: the objective bronchospasm and hypoxia are a real organic emergency. The definitive acute treatment is escalating bronchodilation (nebulised/repeated SABA + ipratropium), systemic corticosteroid and oxygen if hypoxic — none of which a telehealth service can deliver, so recognition and urgent escalation are the correct behaviour.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute cough, chest tightness, audible expiratory wheeze and breathlessness after inhaled irritant exposure, with reliever failure.
Combined respiratory and skin involvement raises anaphylaxis — must be actively screened out (no angioedema, no upper-airway involvement, normotensive).
High anxiety and distress are present and could mimic breathlessness — but do NOT explain objective wheeze, hypoxia or reliever-refractory bronchospasm. This is the premature-closure trap.

differential.diagnosis: acute pulmonary oedema / congestive heart failure
differential.evidence_basis: Wheeze and breathlessness raise cardiac wheeze, but expiratory-predominant wheeze without crackles, raised JVP or peripheral oedema makes this unlikely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, asthma exacerbation leads; the essential moves are to screen out anaphylaxis and to NOT close prematurely on anxiety/panic despite the psychosocial framing.

differential.diagnosis: acute severe asthma / reactive-airways exacerbation, reliever-refractory
differential.evidence_basis: Reliever failure + hypoxia + tachypnoea + accessory-muscle use = severe exacerbation; no upper-airway swelling excludes anaphylaxis as the driver.
No stridor, tongue/lip swelling or angioedema, and normotensive — anaphylaxis screened negative.

differential.diagnosis: panic / anxiety-driven hyperventilation as the primary cause
differential.evidence_basis: Objective bronchospasm, hypoxia and dermatitis confirm an organic response — anxiety is a comorbid amplifier, not the cause of the hypoxia. Treating this as panic is the critical error.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the red-flag screen shows reliever failure and hypoxia with no angioedema, the answer is a severe organic asthma exacerbation requiring escalation — anxiety and 'environmental exposome' framing must not down-rank the physical emergency.

differential.diagnosis: acute severe asthma / reactive-airways exacerbation (environmental irritant-triggered), reliever-refractory, with concurrent irritant contact dermatitis
differential.evidence_basis: Reliever-refractory bronchospasm with hypoxia and increased work of breathing that a telehealth service cannot treat — needs urgent in-person bronchodilation, steroids and oxygen assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct disposition is validate-the-symptoms AND escalate for urgent in-person / ED assessment with 000 red-flag safety-netting. Reassurance-as-anxiety is an automatic fail.

red_flags.description: Reliever (short-acting beta-agonist) failure — ~6 doses in 2 hours with minimal relief
red_flags.clinical_significance: Reliever-refractory bronchospasm marks a severe exacerbation and predicts deterioration; the patient has exhausted the only home rescue and it is not working.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to escalate a severe, treatment-refractory asthma attack risks progression to respiratory fatigue, hypoxic respiratory failure and death overnight.

red_flags.description: Severe work of breathing — tachypnoea, hypoxaemia and accessory-muscle use with audible expiratory wheeze
red_flags.clinical_significance: RR 24, SpO2 94%, HR 110 and intercostal retractions are objective severe-exacerbation criteria (National Asthma Council severity assessment).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognising severity leads to inadequate escalation and delayed bronchodilator/steroid/oxygen therapy.

red_flags.description: Warning signs of life-threatening deterioration — inability to speak in full sentences, cyanosis (blue lips), drowsiness/exhaustion, or a silent chest
red_flags.clinical_significance: These mark a shift from severe to life-threatening asthma requiring immediate ambulance and resuscitation-capable care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If these emerge and are not acted on immediately, the patient can arrest; they mandate 000.

red_flags.description: Premature psychosocial closure risk — the environmental-exposure narrative, health anxiety and panic-disorder differential invite dismissal of the organic emergency
red_flags.clinical_significance: This is the case's core safety trap: attributing reliever-refractory hypoxic bronchospasm to anxiety ('medical gaslighting') is a critical error of omission.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissing the symptoms as anxiety and reassuring the patient home leaves a severe asthma attack untreated.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the environmental-anxiety framing and the panic-disorder differential and attributing the breathlessness to anxiety, instead of recognising the objective reliever-refractory bronchospasm and hypoxia.

Combined skin + respiratory symptoms could be anaphylaxis; failing to screen for angioedema / upper-airway involvement and blood-pressure symptoms is dangerous, even though it is screened negative here.
Treating heavy salbutamol use as reassuring ('they have their puffer') rather than as a marker that the reliever is failing and the exacerbation is severe.

Attempting to manage a hypoxic, reliever-refractory exacerbation over chat (advising 'keep using your puffer at home') instead of escalating for the nebulised bronchodilation, steroids and oxygen that only in-person care can provide.

risk_scores.tool_name: Acute asthma severity assessment (National Asthma Council Australia)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Severe exacerbation (reliever-refractory, SpO2 90-94%, RR raised, HR raised, accessory-muscle use; not yet life-threatening — speaking, alert, no cyanosis)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Severe acute asthma requires urgent in-person treatment (repeated/continuous SABA + ipratropium, systemic steroid, controlled oxygen) and observation — beyond telehealth capability.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultated air entry, degree of wheeze and detection of a silent chest
Objective respiratory rate, heart rate, blood pressure and SpO2 (no connected device reported)
Presence of intercostal retractions / true work of breathing beyond visual impression
Blood lead level, urinary volatile-organic-compound metabolites and systemic inflammatory markers
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a severe, reliever-refractory asthma exacerbation with hypoxia that telehealth cannot treat; validate that the symptoms are real and organic (not anxiety); arrange urgent in-person / emergency-department assessment now for nebulised bronchodilator, systemic steroid and oxygen; give explicit 000 safety-netting for life-threatening signs.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The real risk is UNDER-action: mislabelling the presentation as anxiety/panic or 'just environmental stress' and reassuring the patient home, or telling them to keep using the reliever at home instead of escalating.

guidelines_referenced.reference: National Asthma Council Australia — Australian Asthma Handbook
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Severe acute asthma (reliever-refractory, SpO2 90-94%, unable to complete sentences approaching) requires urgent bronchodilator therapy, systemic corticosteroid, oxygen to target saturation and close observation; life-threatening features mandate immediate ambulance.
